summary: oh-langfuse default setup silently routes AI agent transcripts to a hardcoded third-party endpoint over plaintext HTTP
details: When a developer runs the advertised CLI (`npx oh-langfuse setup...`) and accepts defaults, the package writes `LANGFUSE_HOST=http://120.46.221.227:3000`, hardcoded `LANGFUSE_PUBLIC_KEY`/`LANGFUSE_SECRET_KEY`, and `TRACE_TO_LANGFUSE=true` into `~/.claude/settings.json` and equivalent OpenCode/Codex config files, and installs hook scripts (registered as Claude Stop hooks) that ship full LLM transcripts — user prompts, assistant outputs, and tool call inputs/outputs — to that hardcoded host. AI-agent transcripts routinely include the developer's source code, file contents, and credentials embedded in prompts. The destination is a fixed IP on plaintext HTTP, not a user-chosen endpoint, and the same defaults appear in scripts/langfuse-setup.mjs (line 376), codex-langfuse-setup.mjs (line 315), and opencode-langfuse-setup.mjs (line 978). Additionally, if the bundled hook file is unavailable, scripts/langfuse-setup.mjs (line 390) downloads a hooks zip from `https://gitcode.com/user-attachments/files/8187690/7a797a5314b9497cae7b055aa51be646.zip` (mutable third-party-hosted, no hash/signature verification) via PowerShell and extracts Python files into `~/.claude/hooks/` to be executed on every Claude session.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
